sentences.text: Good afternoon.
Same to you.
I was born on November 1st, 1907.
Full 88.
I'm in my 89th year of age.
In a city, Barisaw.
It was-- at that time, it was Russia, and it is still Russia.
It is west-- or east, approximately 40, 50 miles east of Minsk, as the bigger city, and then somewhere 100 miles or maybe more west of Smolensk.
My father's Jewish name was Moishe.
Moses.
Moses, usually.

He was a whole-- wholesalesman, what was considered, I think, in Barisaw, it was the only business on wholesale in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wheat and corn and-- and whatever it is, you know, all connected with food, [INAUDIBLE] and food.
He with our other two brothers, it was so-called, all right-- three brothers managed this business and they were very wealthy.
Yes.
My, my father?
My father-- he-- he is a son of Hasidic-- Hasidic Lubavitch Jew, you know, person.
My father's name, I told you.
His father?
David.
I'm after my grandfather.
My grandfather died before I was born.
Hasidic Jew.
My father was a religious Jew.
Still, you know, he told me he was a Lubavitcher [?
Hassad. ?] Although, he didn't have any beard and short hair.
And-- but he was going, as I remember, to a Hasidic shul.
Special-- special Hasidic-- in my early years, when I was six, seven years, eight years, I was going with him almost every day in the morning to the synagogue.
Only one, on one side.
Mother was born in a city where only 20 Jewish families were living, in a city called Vyazma.
You can compare to the United States, Plymouth, Massachusetts.
It is a old, old Russian city.
Schlindman.
Anna or [?
Channa. ?]
At that time, it was-- Jews were permitted, at that time, to come to this area under few conditions-- if he is a wholesale [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] businessman, if he's a lawyer, if he's a doctor.
My [? father ?] was a wholesale business in-- in-- we-- we-- the name was colonial products.
But it is not colonial-- it was all possible, you know.
But one of the brothers was every day at the railroad station where wagons were unloaded.
Let's see.
Wheat--
--could be from-- from Kiev.
From-- different color rice.
Rice was all right.
Rice.
But all the other foodstuffs were coming mostly from Ukraine or from center-- center-- central Russia.
No.
No.
My father, my grandfather-- grandfather on the mother's side was a-- basically, he was a simple worker, otherwise he wouldn't-- he was working in a shoe business.
Otherwise, he wouldn't be allowed to-- to be in Vyasma.
Synagogue in Vyasma, it is a 20 Jewish families.
It was a small Jewish-- on the top of a old house.
One small room, where once a week, the Jews would gather for prayers.
During the week, no prayers.
I had only one sister.
Liza.
She's still alive.
She's one-- 12-- 14 months older than I am.
She lives now in Israel.
In Jerusalem.
Home-- at the beginning-- you must understand, at the-- at the beginning I was going to a cheder.
It was somewhere about six years I was going to a cheder.
And I was in cheder approximately two years or, say, so.
And on-- after two years, I revolted.
My mother was not there.
My grandmother in Vyazma--
My grandmother in Vyazma was very sick with a pneumonia, and she went, you know, to Vyazma.
Only my father was there at home.
And I revolted, and I said I'm not going to cheder.
I wanted to go, you know, to-- to go-- start to go-- thinking about going to a gymnasium.
Eight-- eight and a half years.
I revolted.
It is the only time that my father came.
He gave me a spanking.
I went for one day to the cheder.
And I came back, and I say, I'm not going.
Year my grandfather came-- probably some-- somehow my father informed-- my grandmother came and took me away to Vyazma.
Just to go away from Barisaw, from this-- from this, oh, you know, form of life.
You know, he took to me to-- to Vyazma.
And my-- my mother was in Vyazma.

I stayed for eight, nine months in Vyazma-- almost-- and I was making preparations to enter a Russian-- it is called gymnasia-- eight year, from 10 years admitted, to approximately to [? 80 ?] years, you know, age.
It was a government-- government school, government school, yes.
There were very-- in this-- in this [INAUDIBLE]-- maybe in the-- in Vyazma, maybe three or four Jews were in a whole-- the school.
When I came back to Barisaw, Barisaw had a very large such gymnasia, you know, which, in each class, three-- three-- three equal-- equal classes.
And there, I took my test, and I was admitted to the gymnasium-- gymnasia in Barisaw.
I was there only for one year.
Here, it was the time-- it was already a revolution, or already time, [INAUDIBLE] beginning the German occupation but--
In 1918, in-- in-- the Germans had to-- went-- or went already back to Germany, it was already after the war, and approximately the time of-- of the war.

And-- and-- and my parents were very much afraid if the Bolsheviks are coming, they will be killed, because they were the second-richest people in the-- in the city, and another had a factory of-- a big factory.
We moved, basically, to Kiev.
Kiev is the capital of Ukraine.
For one year, I was going to school there.
And then, you know, again, became the-- the place became too hot for my parents.
And we decided all to move out of Russia, and we moved and finally landed in a city called Bialystok.
Bialystok was Poland.
And here we stayed for approximately 20-some years.
I started school.
I started school, and I was [? cut out ?] in a private school which is considered inferior.
The best schools were the government schools, considered.
The government schools were the best, and it was very hard for a Jew to enter such a government school.
So Jewish-- Jewish-- at the beginning, Jews had to go to private schools.
There were three private such Jewish gymnasia in-- in-- in Bialystok.
That is called for the three teachers, the [?
Zeligman, ?] [?
Liebenhaft, ?] [?
Iderich ?] and [?
Dereginski, ?] that is the owners of the school.
It was on-- on-- how it was?
It is on the main-- we'll say on the main street of-- in Bialystok.
I don't remember [INAUDIBLE].
I was very much-- I was very much involved in soccer.
I was very good.
Oh, it was-- at that time, I was 12 years of age-- you know, with-- with-- from other schools, from-- we-- we played every day.
Non-Jews, together.
Yes.
And--
We-- in Bialystok, I was [? 12. ?] And I saw that our school did not give a-- a diploma to enter a university.
I left the school, and I worked with my friend together with a teacher, and we took the test to a Polish government school, and we were admitted.
It was-- the main school was in a small city called Drohiczyn.
You want to spell?
D-R-O-H-I-C-A-- C-Z-Y-N, on-- on the River Bug.
And that was-- and we were in a-- among 200, 200 students or 300, we were 20 Jews there.
It was-- no, that was high school.
High school.
There, I will not say whether it was anti-Semitism.
Sure, you know, we were separate.
We and the Poles were separated, but we didn't experience.
As a matter of fact, my parents were, at that time, very poor.
Happened because, you know, there was all the money.
They kept the money in the-- in the rubles.
And it was completely devaluated, and from the richest family, poorest.
[INAUDIBLE] money-- [INAUDIBLE] money, which don't have any value.
My mother, basically, my mother opened a small, we'll say, restaurant, only dinner-- dinner time.
And it became very popular.
She was basically a very excellent cook.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
In Bialystok, yes.
And the-- and she was, [INAUDIBLE], you know, which was for a while.
It was not very, we'll see, respected, you know, as far as my parents concerned.
You know, I had problems with this.
No, you know, I-- I was assisting-- assisting a girl, you know, and the girl, we were-- we'll say, in love.
But in the last moment, she said, you know, my parents were glad.
I was already in medical school.
I was already in my fifth year of medical school, and she said my parents were glad they saw you as a-- as a-- my husband, but your parents.
They were-- they have a factory-- factory in Bialystok from making-- making wool-- woolen material, you know.
How you call it?
Yes.
And-- and I didn't forget.
Yes.
Sure, she believed, but at this time, you know, in Bialystok, she's poor.
What?
I entered a-- I graduated from high school in, uh, '26 as number-one student.
Straight A's.
I applied to the University of Vilna, nevertheless, with straight A's, and I was not accepted.
Because I'm Jew.
There was, you know, a scandal.
The dean was taking money, admitting Jews not with excellent--
Grades.
And I was not admitted.
And then it was-- came into the open, and the next dean was very strict.
I-- we had-- we had to take examinations.
I take the entrance examinations, and with my tests, my-- my--
From-- from the school, you know, I was admitted in 1927 to the medical school in Vilna.
There, you know, you didn't go first to pre-med.
You would graduate medical school, and it is six years lasting medical school.
It is-- Lithuania, it was at that time, Poland.
It was at that time Poland.
It was the closest-- the closest to me where I lived in Bialystok, the closest was Vilna.
I had relatives.
I could live there, you know, somehow sustain.
It was, you know, it was problem with sustain.
I have relatives there.
They helped me when I was there.
Yeah.
Oh, it was, at that time, the most respected, you know, profession for a Jewish-- for a Jewish young boy, you know, a young guy.
But--
Right now.
I don't consider.
I will not advise my-- I'm glad that my sons didn't go into medicine, you know.

Medicine is now here in the United States, with malpractices business, it's in the dog house, especially my specialty, obstetrics and gynecology, which was before, very, very respected-- respected became one of the worst specialties.
Yeah, sure.
They help me very much but how-- as much they could.
Well, you know, they-- they had a small business, and they helped me with very little money.
I had to-- to-- to be a teacher, you know, be tutoring-- tutoring to make-- to make my money in Vilna.

Oh, you know, not salary-- everything from [INAUDIBLE] the high school and the-- not good in mathematics, fine, not good in-- in Polish language, fine, not good in Latin language, fine-- everything, you know.
Latin was one of the most important languages.
I-- training.
I was-- six year--
My mother's brother from Vyazma, too, [? won-- ?] went to-- entered the gymnasia and then, you know, was admitted to Moscow medical school.
And he became a doctor, and-- and he became an obstetrician-gynecologist.
And then somehow, influenced-- oh, my uncle is in obstetrics, let me go and be in obstetrics.
That is-- that is number one, and then, you know, just simple life.
Life dictated.
I graduated.
I had to go for one year to Polish Army as a doctor.
Yes.
I was involved in all the--
All the-- all the years-- all the years I was involved.
In Vilna, I was involved in-- in-- it was called, you know, the Union of Jewish Medical Students.
In Polish.
No, in Polish it was-- it was [?
Radovski ?] Club Medical.
Something like this.
Because the Polish didn't-- didn't accept us.
And we had somehow had representation for the-- before the dean and-- and professors.
So you have to have a representation.
So we organize as a Jewish.
And then maybe it was-- maybe the Poles-- the dean advised make-- make your own organization.
Educational, in the first-- [? first of all. ?] I was the-- I was the librarian at the beginning, and I was buying books, medical books for everyone.
How we've got money, we would organize balls.
You know, balls.
A ball, you know, a--
Dance, and [? dance ?] it was usually, the best, usually became the best usual dance evening in Vilna.
No, it was basically not Jewish.
We wouldn't come.
[INAUDIBLE] they were completely-- completely away from-- the Jewish.
Many didn't talk to us.
I will not tell restrict.
No, the professors were very-- very equal to all-- to all.
But it was-- we were sitting on the left side in the-- left side in the--
Yes.
And they were sitting on the right side.
They were-- they were 90 people.
We were 25, 30, but that was how the division.
We did-- didn't dare to go to their side, and they will not go to our side.
Correct.
Now, the first year of medicine is-- one of the main subjects is anatomy or histology, you know, microscopic anatomy.
But for anatomy, you have to have cadavers to study, to-- to study all the organs and how it is built.
The Polish students brought the conditions that they would not allow us to work on their Christian cadavers.
Christian cadavers.
So we had to get-- to get-- you know, to get-- when we went to Jewish hospital, they said, yes, from time to time, we have, you know, a poor man without a family died.
Nobody-- nobody takes-- [INAUDIBLE] took care.
We went-- but you see, the religious Jews would come right away and will take and give him a decent burial.
So we went, you know, to-- to the Rabbi of Vilna.
And the Rabbi was Chaim Eizer Rodianski.
I had met him in his-- in his-- where he lived, where he was teaching.
I wouldn't know.
I presume so.
That is on the-- on [?
Villenska ?] Street, yeah, called [?
Villenska ?] Street.
And--
What?
The main rabbi, yes.
He was the main rabbi.
We came and asked him, Rabbi, help us, Jewish students to get-- to get a cadaver.
He said, please, no, you must understand-- he was very human-- you must understand me.
Said, I would like to help you as much as possible.
But this is a Jewish law that if a Jew died, within 24 hours, he has to be interred, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I cannot help you.
What?
We came in-- oh, three-- three, from the 20-something Jew-- Jews, we-- I was the leading.
Kapinski ?] was with me.
And-- and-- I forgot-- from a very famous Jewish-- Jewish-- from a Jewish family very famous in Vilna, she was the third one.
I-- maybe I recall her name.
You know, it is-- it is just-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]
And then, after we finished our conversation, we said, rabbi, thank you very much.
He turned to us [INAUDIBLE] stretched the hand, shake-- shook hands with me, shook hands with doctor-- doctor-- with-- at that time, [?
Tom ?] [?
Kapinski, ?] and they-- and the other--
She stretches her hand, and he makes 180 degree turn at the side.
And [?
Kapinski, ?] who knew more about religion, put her hand down, and we left, and he told her that a rabbi will never give a hand to a woman.
He didn't give us any suggestions.
I made many suggestions.
Nevertheless, maybe indirect, nevertheless, in Jewish hospital, one died.

We can-- we-- we-- we got this-- this body, and we had a body to-- but we didn't have-- so we needed at least, four or five bodies, you know, but it is with only one body, so 20 some, one would section the arms, the other with the-- the-- the legs, and the other the bowels, and the other--
Finished, you brought back, you know, brought back to a burial.
To a Jewish burial.
Yes, it was-- I was accepted.
I was not accepted-- I was called to go for one year to the Polish army, after I graduated from medical school.
Oh, I was, you know, [INAUDIBLE] pleased.
I would-- I like to go straight, you know, in-- into my profession.
But if I had to go for one year to the army, I went to the army.
It was in-- in-- in-- in Warsaw.

It was a special medical-- military medical school for professional doctor-- for professional military doctors, and-- and we were all a part of this for only [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we were called to duty.
What?
We-- we went--
I was happier in the school, medical school, this military medical school, where we-- we had a-- from one side, simple medical-- simple military education.
And the-- and the-- besides we had lectures on military medicine.
Yes, the students were already-- we were-- Jews were already only around 10 in 120.
Something-- we have to stop?
